title: Weaving heart monitoring into daily life

Shingo Tsukada (left) and Kei Kuwabara discuss technologies for continuous heart monitoring.Credit: NTT Cardiovascular diseases are one of the leading causes of mortality worldwide. Rising rates of heart failure, especially in super-ageing societies such as Japan’s, are making early detection and monitoring increasingly important.The electrocardiogram (ECG) is widely regarded as a gold standard for monitoring the heart. But with a substantial portion of all serious heart attacks in Japan occurring outside of hospitals, it is important to take ECG technology beyond clinics to enable reliable, long-term monitoring.That is the challenge that researchers at telecommunications company NTT, in collaboration with engineers at the advanced-materials company Toray, based in Tokyo, Japan, have taken up. Specifically, they want to bring clinical-grade monitoring into everyday life by integrating advances in materials, sensing and data science.“We believe that by developing long-term ECG monitoring, we can contribute to the early diagnosis of heart disease,” says Shingo Tsukada, a biomedical engineer at NTT Basic Research Laboratories in Kanagawa.A wearable ECG fabricAt the heart of this technology is a wearable fabric that acts as an electrode and picks up ECG signals1. This fabric, called hitoe, originates from research on brain–machine interfaces, where a hydrophilic, flexible conductive polymer was combined with fibres as an alternative to conventional metal electrodes. The high biocompatibility of the fabric led researchers to believe it could have another use: as a continuous sensor on the surface of the skin.Building on this idea, the technology was translated into textile-based bioelectrodes and, through collaboration with Toray, developed into a wearable fabric suitable for use in clothing.Importantly, its hydrophilic characteristics retain skin moisture — a key factor in maintaining stable electrical contact with the skin for reliable ECG acquisition. By embedding sensing capability directly into fabric, this approach helped pioneer wearable bioelectronic monitoring in Japan, extending ECG measurements from clinical settings into everyday life.But the fabric is only one element of a comprehensive system. ECG waveforms vary significantly between individuals, making it difficult to detect abnormal signals, and most ECG readings still need to be interpreted by doctors.To address this, Tsukada has developed an analytical framework termed ‘tensor cardiography’2, which transforms ECG signals into structured mathematical representations. “By representing each heartbeat as a time-series distribution described by multiple parameters, we can capture essential features of cardiac activity in a more compact and interpretable form,” Tsukada explains. This framework enables both efficient data compression and systematic analysis of continuously acquired ECG signals, providing a way to identify subtle deviations associated with cardiac abnormalities.Integrating dataThe third key component is integration with digital infrastructure. A compact low-power transmitter designed for use with the fabric continuously streams physiological data to a smartphone, where it is securely relayed to NTT’s cloud-based platform. Leveraging NTT’s advanced communications and data infrastructure, the system enables reliable, real-time access for cardiologists, supporting continuous remote monitoring.“Access to specialist care can be limited, particularly in rural areas in Japan,” says Kei Kuwabara, a research engineer at NTT’s Device Innovation Center in Kanagawa. “Using our system, data collected during rehabilitation exercises can be streamed seamlessly in real time to specialists at distant hospitals.”In this way, measurements taken in everyday settings can be shared instantly with specialists, opening new possibilities for remote diagnosis and continuous care beyond hospital walls.With sensing, analytics and connectivity now integrated, this system is enabling more continuous and accessible cardiovascular care, Kuwabara believes. “Through innovation and collaboration with partner companies, we’re driving digital transformation in healthcare and contributing to the early detection and prevention of cardiovascular disease,” he says.
Learn more about the wearable biosensor developed by NTT and Toray.
